StormOutlineSection.description: A section in the STORM-generated outline.

search_keywords.description: Comma-separated domain-specific keywords for routing evidence to this section. Include technical terms, units, method names. E.g. 'epoxy, MPa, dolly, ASTM D4541, cohesive failure'
search_keywords.title: Search Keywords

evidence_summary.description: Summary of evidence from conversations for this section
evidence_summary.title: Evidence Summary

perspectives.description: STORM perspectives that contributed to this section
perspectives.title: Perspectives

sections.description: Ordered sections for the research report

schema_json.title: StormOutlinePlan

prompt:
You are a research outline architect. Based on multi-perspective expert interviews, create a comprehensive report outline.

RESEARCH QUESTION: What are the proven health benefits and risks of intermittent fasting?

INTERVIEW CONVERSATIONS (5 perspectives, 5 total Q&A rounds):

=== Scientific (Dr. Krista Varady) ===
  Q: In randomized controlled trials that match total energy intake between intermittent fasting (16:8 time-restricted eating or alternate-day fasting) and continuous energy restriction, does intermittent 
  A: The provided search results converge on a broad finding but disagree on specifics. Most randomized trials and pooled analyses of IF (alternate-day fasting, 5:2, and continuous IER) against CER show SIMILAR weight loss when energy intake is matched [Source 1, 2, 7, 8, 9]. Harvie et al.'s foundational work (Source 9) reported equivalent IER and CER weight loss (LOCF analysis) with comparable confidence intervals. A 2-year real-world dietary setting (Source 7) likewise found ICR and CCR equivalent 
  Key findings: Multiple SRs and RCTs find IF and CER produce similar weight loss under matched energy intake condit; Lowe 2020 TREAT trial: no significant between-group weight loss difference for 16:8 TRE vs consisten; Chow 2020: TRE did not cause trunk/arm lean mass loss; non-significant effects on fat mass, lean mas; Frontiers in Nutrition meta-analysis suggested iER superior to cER for body composition with mean we; Outlier Gavin Publishers paper (Source 5) claims CR produced no weight loss, contradicting the prepo

=== Regulatory (Dr. Andrea J. MacNeill) ===
  Q: What evidence thresholds would FDA, EFSA, and Health Canada require to approve an authorized health claim for intermittent fasting (e.g., 'may aid weight management' or 'may improve glycemic control')
  A: The search results document the general regulatory frameworks for health claims at FDA (Sources 1-4, 6-8, 10) and EFSA (Sources 11-15), but contain limited IF-specific substantiation analysis. FDA's Authorized Health Claims require 'significant scientific agreement' (SSA) among qualified experts, a high bar codified in 21 CFR Part 101 Subpart E (Sources 1, 3, 10). A lower-tier Qualified Health Claim path exists where evidence is 'supported by scientific evidence but does not meet the more rigoro
  Key findings: FDA SSA standard: 'significant scientific agreement among qualified experts' codified in 21 CFR Part; FDA Qualified Health Claim tier available with disclaimers for evidence below SSA (Sources 2, 6, 7).; EFSA disease-risk-reduction claims governed by Regulation EC 1924/2006; require beneficial alteratio; No IF-specific SSA, QHC, or EFSA opinion document surfaced in the search results.; Lay coverage (Source 9) characterizes IF evidence as contested; Source 5 describes general periodic-

=== Industry (Mark Haub) ===
  Q: What do published effectiveness, adherence, and retention data from commercial intermittent-fasting mobile applications (Zero, Fastic, LIFE Fasting Tracker, Simple, DoFasting) actually show for real-w
  A: The search results include two published real-world IF app studies (Torres 2022 and Huntriss 2024 on SIMPLE) plus marketing and review material. The Torres 2022 study (Sources 1, 2, 4, 11), a 52-week observational study of two commercial IF mobile apps, reported that by week 26 users lost over 1% of starting weight per Eligible Fasting Hour per day on average (Source 4). Huntriss 2024 (Sources 3, 12) found that SIMPLE app engagement was directly associated with weight loss, and Valinskas 2023 (S
  Key findings: Torres 2022 (n reported in source): by week 26, users of two IF apps lost >1% of starting weight per; Huntriss 2024: SIMPLE app engagement is directly associated with weight loss in adults with overweig; Real-world clinical TRE (Source 7): modest but significant reductions in weight, BMI, and waist circ; Marketing claims (Sources 5, 9) cite large individual-level losses not representative of average app; No sources provide direct head-to-head effect-size comparisons between app-user cohorts and peer-rev

=== Economic (Dr. Alison Galvani) ===
  Q: What published cost-effectiveness analyses exist for intermittent fasting as a weight-management or glycemic-control intervention, and what incremental cost-effectiveness ratios (cost per QALY or per 
  A: The most directly relevant source is a trial-based and lifetime-model cost-utility study of a fasting-mimicking diet programme in type-2-diabetes patients (Sources 1, 4, 13). That study determined cost-effectiveness of a one-year monthly 5-consecutive-day fasting-mimicking-diet programme added to diabetes standard care, and is identified as 'the first to evaluate the costs and consequences of adding the FMD fasting nutri-technology to diabetes standard care' (Source 13). However, specific ICER v
  Key findings: One trial-based + lifetime-model cost-utility analysis exists for a fasting-mimicking diet programme; Non-pharmacologic diet/exercise QALY cost ranges $25,400-$57,400 (AJMC, Source 3) — baseline for cos; The Nutritious Eating with Soul Study (Source 6): 8% weight loss at 2 years, $1,704-$7,889 per QALY ; NICE dietary intervention cost per QALY £16,794 (Source 7); telehealth passive $7,084/QALY (Source 1; No head-to-head cost-effectiveness comparison of IF counseling vs GLP-1 pharmacotherapy exists in th

=== Public_Health (Dr. Katherine Zeratsky) ===
  Q: What does the existing evidence say about who should NOT follow intermittent fasting — specifically safety signals and contraindications in pregnant or lactating individuals, adolescents, shift worker
  A: The search results document multiple population-specific safety concerns for IF. For pregnancy, Source 1 reports IF (including Ramadan fasting) during pregnancy was associated with a 'minor but statistically significant' reduction in neonatal birth weight; Source 8 notes birth weight and preterm risk were not significantly affected but placental weight was significantly lower with fasting; Sources 6 and 10 list IF contraindications in pregnancy (nutritional deficiency risk, possible preterm birt
  Key findings: IF in pregnancy (including Ramadan fasting): minor but statistically significant reduction in neonat; Preclinical: CR and IF during lactation caused marked undernutrition in offspring persistent through; Sulfonylurea users: documented severe relapsing sulphonylurea-induced hypoglycemia cases (Sources 11; T2DM: 9-hour TRE appears safe for up to 5 days/week in a small study with glucose monitoring (Source; Evidence gaps: no direct sources surfaced for adolescents, shift workers as a distinct cohort, or IF

PERSPECTIVES COVERED: Economic, Regulatory, Public_Health, Industry, Scientific

INSTRUCTIONS:
1. Identify 8-15 thematic clusters across ALL conversations.
2. For each cluster, create a report section with:
   - title: Clear, descriptive section heading
   - description: 2-3 sentences on what this section should cover
   - evidence_summary: Key findings from conversations that belong here
   - perspectives: Which STORM perspectives contributed to this section
   - order: Sequential position (1-based)
3. Sections should be ordered logically: background -> methodology -> findings -> analysis -> implications -> future directions.
4. Each section should draw from at least 2 different perspectives where possible (multi-perspective synthesis).
5. Do NOT create a section for every single Q&A pair — cluster related information together.
6. Ensure COMPLETE COVERAGE: every key finding from the interviews should appear in at least one section's evidence_summary.

Return a JSON object with a 'sections' array of outline section objects.
